findings.title: Registration consent is required in the UI but not recorded server-side
findings.description: The registration form requires a privacy-policy checkbox, but the submitted payload contains only name, email, password, and preferredVersion, and the backend profile record stores no consent timestamp, policy version, or consent scope. That leaves the service unable to evidence that consent was freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
findings.evidence: The checkbox at lines 178-183 is mandatory, but the POST body at lines 71-76 omits any consent fields. The backend profile written in lambda/api/index.mjs lines 416-431 stores email, name, passwordHash, salt, preferredVersion, emailVerified, and createdAt only.
findings.fix: Add explicit consent fields to the register payload, store consent scope, timestamp, and policy/version identifiers in DynamoDB, and expose withdrawal/preference management for any optional processing tied to consent.

findings.title: Third-party analytics loads before any consent or preference flow
findings.description: The root layout injects Plausible analytics for every visitor as soon as the app loads. The repository contains no consent banner, analytics preference center, or conditional loading path tied to user choice. Even for cookieless analytics, this creates a GDPR transparency and lawful-basis gap unless the operator has documented and disclosed a defensible alternative basis outside the codebase.
findings.evidence: layout.tsx lines 66-67 load plausible.io globally. A repo-wide search found no cookie banner or consent-management implementation under src/app, src/components, or src/lib.
findings.fix: Implement a consent/preferences layer for analytics and gate Plausible script injection on that preference, or remove third-party analytics for regulated users and update privacy disclosures accordingly.

findings.title: The backend supports account deletion, but the app exposes no user-facing erasure flow
findings.description: The API provides an authenticated DELETE /api/account route that removes account data, but the Settings page exposes sign-out, backup, import/export, and password-change controls only. There is no in-product delete-account or erase-my-data path, which makes the right to erasure materially harder to exercise.
findings.evidence: The account UI at lines 223-245 shows profile details and a sign-out button only. The deletion endpoint exists in lambda/api/index.mjs at lines 363 and 847-905, but there is no client-side call to /api/account in the repository.
findings.fix: Add a delete-account flow in Settings that requires password re-entry, calls DELETE /api/account, clears local data, and confirms what data will be removed.

findings.title: No retention limit is applied to persisted account data
findings.description: The DynamoDB table supports TTL only through the expiresAt attribute, but user profile, study, journal, bookmark, and daily records are written without any retention field. The repo contains no cleanup job or retention-policy implementation for personal data stored in the account backend.
findings.evidence: User content is stored in lambda/api/index.mjs lines 1001-1015, 1092-1130, 1183-1195, and 1260-1271 without expiresAt. terraform/dynamodb.tf lines 38-41 enable TTL only for records that explicitly include expiresAt.
findings.fix: Define retention periods by data type, store expiry metadata where appropriate, and add scheduled purge logic or lifecycle jobs. This requires business and legal input before code changes.

findings.title: Bearer tokens and user profile data are persisted in localStorage
findings.description: Authentication tokens, user profile data, and cached encryption metadata are stored in browser localStorage. Because localStorage is readable by any injected JavaScript, a future XSS defect or compromised third-party script would expose active sessions and user identifiers. This is weaker than an HttpOnly session-cookie model for a production consumer app processing account and journal data.
findings.evidence: auth-context.tsx stores bible_token, bible_user, and selah_enc_cache in localStorage at lines 19-21, 25-31, 49-56, and 62-71. StudyChat also reads bible_token from localStorage at src/components/StudyChat.tsx lines 136-143.
findings.fix: Move session handling to Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookies or another server-managed session mechanism, and keep only strictly necessary non-sensitive state in web storage.

findings.title: Account backup uploads journal content in plaintext when the encryption key is unavailable
findings.description: The account-backup path encrypts journals only when a master key is present. If the user is signed in but the in-memory key is unavailable, the backup routine sends the raw journal entry to the API, and the backend stores plaintext content. That defeats the stated zero-knowledge journal design for a common recovery state.
findings.evidence: account-sync.ts lines 107-115 send the unmodified journal entry when masterKey is falsy. Settings calls backupLocalDataToAccount(undefined, masterKey) at src/app/settings/page.tsx lines 139-152, so a signed-in user can trigger backup even when masterKey is null.
findings.fix: Block journal backup until the journal key is unlocked, or split backup so journals are skipped with a clear warning unless encryption is active. On the backend, reject plaintext journal writes for accounts that have encryption enabled.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe inline and eval execution
findings.description: The CloudFront security headers policy sets a CSP, but it permits both 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src. Those exceptions materially weaken XSS defenses, especially because the frontend also stores bearer tokens in browser storage and loads third-party scripts.
findings.evidence: The CSP at terraform/cloudfront.tf lines 218-220 is: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io.
findings.fix: Remove unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, migrate any inline bootstrapping to nonce- or hash-based scripts, and confirm the build/runtime path works without eval-dependent tooling in production.

findings.title: Privacy notice omits multiple mandatory GDPR disclosures
findings.description: The published privacy policy describes broad product categories and retention goals, but it does not disclose the controller's full identity details, lawful basis per processing activity, supervisory-authority complaint rights, transfer safeguards, whether provision of data is contractual/statutory, or any Article 13 notice specific to the site's actual analytics vendor. That leaves users without the minimum transparency GDPR requires before form submission or site analytics processing.
findings.evidence: The codyjo.com section lists collected data and retention only, and the later rights/transfer sections are limited to generic statements: lines 127-149, 298-333, and 337-355 contain no lawful-basis mapping, no EU/UK complaint-right notice, and no transfer safeguard details.
findings.fix: Expand the privacy page to name the controller, map each processing activity to a lawful basis, identify all recipients including Plausible, explain transfer safeguards for US processing, state complaint rights, and clarify whether each form field is required to respond to the request.

findings.title: Demo request flow collects consent in the UI but does not transmit or record it
findings.description: The demo page requires a privacy checkbox before submission, but the payload sent to the backend omits both the checkbox state and any consent timestamp or policy version. This makes the form's consent language non-verifiable and prevents the operator from proving what the user agreed to.
findings.evidence: The form requires `<input type="checkbox" id="privacyConsent" ... required>` at lines 833-838, but the request payload built at lines 919-927 only sends placeholder identity fields and email; no `privacy_consent`, `consent_timestamp`, or policy version is included before `fetch(`${API_BASE}/demo/request`, ...)` at lines 930-934.
findings.fix: Send explicit consent metadata in the demo API payload, including consent boolean, timestamp, form version/privacy-policy version, and the specific processing scope. Align the legal basis and UI copy with what the backend stores.

findings.title: Contact-form consent records are not versioned or scoped
findings.description: The homepage contact form sends `privacy_consent: true` and a timestamp, but no privacy-policy version, notice text version, or purpose identifier. That is weak evidence for accountability because the site cannot later prove which notice or processing description the visitor agreed to at submission time.
findings.evidence: The POST body at lines 352-358 contains only `name`, `email`, `message`, `privacy_consent: true`, and `consent_timestamp`. The checkbox copy at lines 276-280 points to `/privacy`, but no policy version or form version is transmitted with the request.
findings.fix: Include a stable notice version or policy revision identifier in the payload and store it alongside the submission. If contact processing instead relies on legitimate interests or pre-contractual steps, remove the consent framing and replace it with a clear notice.

findings.title: Plausible analytics is loaded globally but not disclosed in the third-party list or legal-basis narrative
findings.description: Every page loads Plausible from `plausible.io`, but the privacy page's third-party section does not mention Plausible or explain the lawful basis for that processing. Even privacy-friendly analytics still require transparent notice and transfer disclosure when an external processor is involved.
findings.evidence: The layout CSP and script tags allow and load `https://plausible.io` at lines 57 and 103-106. The privacy page's third-party list at `src/pages/privacy.astro` lines 298-309 names AWS, Anthropic, Resend, Amazon SES, and Bolls.life, but not Plausible.
findings.fix: Add Plausible to the privacy notice with purpose, data categories, retention/processor information, and the lawful basis relied on. If consent is required in the target jurisdictions, gate the analytics script until consent is granted.

findings.title: Content Security Policy permits inline scripts and styles
findings.description: Both the main Astro layout and the standalone demo page use a CSP that includes `unsafe-inline` for scripts and styles. That weakens XSS defenses and makes CSP substantially less effective as a browser-enforced mitigation.
findings.evidence: The public-site CSP includes `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'` at `src/layouts/Base.astro` line 57. The standalone demo page repeats the same pattern at `public/demo.html` line 9.
findings.fix: Move inline scripts/styles into static files or nonce/hash them, then remove `unsafe-inline` from both `script-src` and `style-src`. Keep the demo page inside the Astro pipeline so one hardened policy can be applied consistently.

findings.title: Admin access relies on hardcoded Basic-auth verifier in source code
findings.description: The `admin.codyjo.com` gate uses a Lambda@Edge function with a hardcoded SHA-256 hash of the full Basic Authorization header. That keeps the verifier in source control, provides no MFA or user-level accountability, and makes credential rotation a code change instead of a secrets-management operation.
findings.evidence: The function defines `const expected = 'b88ed5d12ded9506eb53a9c68f247dde9aebdb0ea2673c112781368c13112780';` at line 3 and validates incoming Basic auth headers at lines 25-39. No external secret store, IdP, or MFA control is referenced.
findings.fix: Replace the static Basic-auth gate with an identity provider or at minimum pull the verifier from Secrets Manager/SSM, support rotation without code changes, and require MFA-backed authentication for admin access.

findings.title: Deployment IAM policy grants broad wildcard privileges beyond least privilege
findings.description: The GitHub deploy role policy includes `s3:*` on the admin bucket, `lambda:*` on the edge-auth function, and CloudFront actions against `*`. Those permissions exceed the minimum necessary for routine deployment and infrastructure update paths.
findings.evidence: The deploy policy allows `s3:*` at lines 66-78, broad CloudFront operations on `resources = ["*"]` at lines 80-99, and `lambda:*` at lines 132-144.
findings.fix: Reduce the role to the exact CRUD actions needed for deployment and Terraform apply, scope CloudFront permissions to the specific distribution where possible, and remove service-wide wildcards from S3 and Lambda permissions.

findings.title: Public unauthenticated API can create, list, inspect, and destroy deployments
findings.description: The serverless UI exposes a Cloud Run API to `allUsers`, and the Flask application implements no authentication or authorization checks on the deployment endpoints. Any internet user who knows the URL can trigger infrastructure builds, list deployment metadata, retrieve deployment outputs, and request destruction of completed environments.
findings.evidence: frontend/infra/main.tf grants `roles/run.invoker` to `allUsers` for the API service (lines 191-197). frontend/api/main.py exposes POST `/api/deployments`, GET `/api/deployments`, GET `/api/deployments/<deployment_id>`, and POST `/api/deployments/<deployment_id>/destroy` without any auth checks (lines 154-287). frontend/web/app.js calls those endpoints directly from the browser (lines 133-137, 161-162, 209-210, 278-279).
findings.fix: Require authenticated access to Cloud Run and enforce application-level authorization. Restrict invokers to an allowlisted identity group, add signed identity or IAP/OIDC-based access, and scope list/detail/destroy operations to the authenticated user or admin role.

findings.title: CORS is configured as fully permissive on the public API
findings.description: The Flask API enables CORS with default settings, which allows any origin to call the API. Because the API is already publicly invokable, this permits arbitrary third-party websites to drive deployment actions from a victim's browser without any origin restrictions or CSRF-like containment.
findings.evidence: frontend/api/main.py initializes the app and immediately calls `CORS(app)` on lines 20-21, with no `origins`, `methods`, or route-specific restrictions.
findings.fix: Restrict CORS to the exact frontend origin(s), limit allowed methods and headers per route, and disable CORS entirely on endpoints that should not be browser-accessible from arbitrary origins.

findings.title: Deployment-control endpoints have no rate limiting or abuse controls
findings.description: The API accepts unauthenticated create, list, detail, and destroy requests but implements no throttling, quotas, or request-size safeguards at the application layer. This makes the service vulnerable to brute-force enumeration, cost-amplification abuse, and denial-of-service conditions against Cloud Build and GCP resources.
findings.evidence: frontend/api/main.py defines the deployment endpoints on lines 154-287 with no rate-limiting middleware, per-IP quotas, or payload-size enforcement. The frontend repeatedly polls detail endpoints every 10 seconds in frontend/web/app.js lines 197-204.
findings.fix: Add API throttling at Cloud Run/API Gateway or in-app middleware, set body-size limits, and enforce stricter quotas on deployment creation and destroy requests. Pair this with authentication so rate limits can be identity-aware.

findings.title: Public web UI has no privacy notice or transparency layer
findings.description: The browser-based deployment interface collects and transmits user-entered deployment metadata, and the hosted service will also process request metadata such as IP addresses through Cloud Run, Cloud Storage, and Cloud Build. The static frontend contains no privacy notice, no footer link, and the repository contains no privacy-policy page or related disclosure artifact.
findings.evidence: frontend/web/index.html contains the full public UI and ends at line 164 with no privacy-policy link or notice. A repository search for `privacy`, `policy`, and `cookie` returned no policy page. README.md describes a browser-based team UI at lines 193-261 but includes no privacy disclosure path.
findings.fix: Publish a privacy notice linked from every page of the web UI. At minimum disclose controller identity, purposes, legal basis, categories of data processed, recipients, retention periods, international transfers, and data-subject rights. If the tool remains strictly internal, document that scope and restrict public exposure accordingly.

findings.title: Deployment metadata and per-deployment secrets have no defined retention or purge lifecycle
findings.description: The system persists deployment metadata and outputs in a versioned GCS bucket and creates a Secret Manager secret for each deployment password. There is no lifecycle rule on the state bucket, no scheduled cleanup for old deployment records, and password secrets are only deleted during successful destroy operations. Demo environments left running or failed deployments can therefore retain associated operational data and secrets indefinitely.
findings.evidence: frontend/infra/main.tf creates the `state` bucket with versioning enabled on lines 57-65 but no lifecycle policy. frontend/api/main.py creates `${deployment_id}-password` secrets on lines 166-183. frontend/builder/entrypoint.sh deletes the password secret only in the destroy path on lines 117-123. README.md states the tool has 'no ongoing lifecycle management' on line 325.
findings.fix: Define and implement retention periods for deployment metadata, outputs, logs, and password secrets. Add GCS lifecycle rules, schedule cleanup of stale deployment records, and expire or destroy deployment-specific secrets automatically after completion or after a short grace period.

findings.title: Public frontend is deployed without security-header controls
findings.description: The static UI is published directly from a public GCS bucket and the infrastructure does not configure a load balancer, CDN policy, or application layer capable of enforcing browser security headers such as CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, or cache controls for sensitive pages.
findings.evidence: frontend/infra/main.tf publishes the frontend bucket publicly to `allUsers` on lines 51-55. frontend/infra/outputs.tf exposes the production URL as `https://storage.googleapis.com/.../index.html` on lines 1-4. frontend/web/index.html includes no meta-based compensating controls beyond basic HTML head tags (lines 1-8).
findings.fix: Serve the frontend behind a layer that can set security headers, such as Cloud Run, a load balancer with Cloud CDN, or another managed edge. Add at least CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options or `frame-ancestors`, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, and cache restrictions for sensitive views.

findings.title: Service accounts are granted broad project-wide privileges instead of least-privilege access
findings.description: The API service account receives project-wide `cloudbuild.builds.editor`, `storage.objectAdmin`, and `secretmanager.admin`. The builder service account receives broad `compute.admin`, `storage.objectAdmin`, and `iam.serviceAccountUser`. Those roles exceed strict minimum access and increase the impact of compromise of either runtime.
findings.evidence: frontend/infra/main.tf assigns `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor`, `roles/storage.objectAdmin`, and `roles/secretmanager.admin` to the API SA on lines 94-105, and `roles/compute.admin`, `roles/storage.objectAdmin`, `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor`, and `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` to the builder SA on lines 107-119.
findings.fix: Replace broad project roles with narrower predefined or custom roles scoped to the specific bucket, specific secrets, and only the GCP actions required for deployment orchestration. Document the permission model and review it periodically.

findings.title: Missing explicit cookie consent mechanism
findings.description: The application uses Plausible Analytics (privacy-friendly) but does not implement a cookie consent banner. While Plausible is GDPR-compliant, GDPR Article 7 requires that consent is freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Without a visible consent notice and opt-in mechanism, informed consent cannot be demonstrated. Article 25 requires privacy by design and default.
findings.evidence: <!-- Privacy-friendly analytics by Plausible --> <script async src='https://plausible.io/js/...'>No consent banner or user opt-in before script loads.
findings.fix: Implement a cookie consent banner before Plausible loads using CookieConsent.js, OneTrust, or Cookiebot. Banner must: (1) Be shown before analytics loads, (2) Explain Plausible clearly, (3) Provide separate opt-in checkbox, (4) Allow users to reject/withdraw consent, (5) Store consent with timestamp. Delay loading Plausible until after user consent is captured.

findings.title: User enumeration vulnerability in login
findings.description: The password verification code in gallery_admin_login() is not timing-safe for email existence checks. If an email exists, the response time differs from non-existent emails (one queries DynamoDB, the other returns immediately). This timing difference enables attackers to enumerate valid emails in the system. While bcrypt.checkpw() is timing-safe, the surrounding logic should use constant-time comparison.
findings.evidence: gallery_admin_login(): Email lookup branch timing differs. If email not found, returns 401 immediately. If found, performs bcrypt.checkpw(). This timing variance enables user enumeration.
findings.fix: Implement constant-time email verification: always perform a dummy bcrypt check even if email not found. Example: if email not found, use bcrypt.checkpw(password, dummy_hash) to consume the same CPU time. Alternatively, return same error message and delay response uniformly.

findings.title: Privacy policy linked externally; incomplete data processing transparency
findings.description: Frontend links to external privacy policy (https://www.codyjo.com/privacy/#analogify) but provides no in-app notices about data collection. Users are not informed: (1) that email will be logged in access_log table, (2) that comment data (email, IP) will be stored, (3) how long data is kept, (4) what their rights are. Article 14 requires transparency in 'concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form'.
findings.evidence: Privacy Policy link is external only; no in-app notice or disclosure at point of email collection.
findings.fix: Add privacy notice modal before email entry: 'Your email will be stored to grant gallery access. See our [Privacy Policy] for details.' Include: what data is collected (email, IP for comments), why (access, communication, abuse prevention), how long it's kept, who can access it. Make privacy policy accessible in-app, not just external.

findings.title: Gallery email access not restricted; no per-gallery allowlist
findings.description: The auth_view handler validates email syntax but does not enforce per-gallery allowlists. Code contains TODO: 'Validate email against a per-gallery allowlist or domain-based access control. Currently any syntactically valid email can request access to any gallery.' Any user with valid email can request access to any gallery by entering their email. This violates data minimization (Art. 5(1)(c)) — email is collected from anyone, not just intended recipients.
findings.evidence: TODO comment explicitly notes missing allowlist enforcement. Email validation only checks syntax, not gallery access authorization.
findings.fix: Implement per-gallery allowlist: (1) Allow photographers to upload CSV of allowed email domains/addresses when creating gallery, (2) Store allowed_emails or allowed_domains in gallery metadata (DynamoDB), (3) On email submission, check if email matches allowlist before issuing JWT, (4) If allowlist empty, require PIN-only access. This ensures only intended recipients can access.

findings.title: Authentication tokens stored in localStorage (XSS vulnerability)
findings.description: Frontend stores JWT tokens in localStorage and admin keys in sessionStorage. localStorage is vulnerable to XSS — any injected JavaScript can read localStorage and steal tokens. localStorage persists across sessions, significantly increasing the breach window. ISO 27001 A.8.2 requires secure session management; storing sensitive tokens in localStorage violates this control.
findings.evidence: localStorage.setItem(`gallery_token_${galleryId}`, authToken); sessionStorage.setItem('gallery_admin_key', key); Any XSS can steal these tokens.
findings.fix: Migrate from localStorage to HttpOnly cookies: Set-Cookie: gallery_token=...; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict; Max-Age=14400. This prevents JavaScript from accessing token, mitigating XSS. Requires backend (Lambda) to set cookies in response. If cookies infeasible, use in-memory storage (JavaScript variable) cleared on page unload.

findings.title: Gallery deletion does not delete S3 photos; manual cleanup required
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) (Storage Limitation), Art. 17 (Right to Erasure)
findings.description: DELETE /admin/gallery/{id} deletes gallery metadata from DynamoDB but does NOT delete S3 photo objects. Response states: 'Associated S3 objects were NOT deleted. Remove manually if desired.' This violates GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) storage limitation — personal photos persist indefinitely after gallery deletion. Violates Art. 17 (right to erasure) — if a client requests deletion, the erasure is incomplete.
findings.evidence: def delete_gallery(): galleries_table.delete_item(...) [S3 objects NOT deleted]; 'Associated S3 objects were NOT deleted. Remove manually if desired.'
findings.fix: Implement automatic cascade deletion: When DELETE /admin/gallery/{id} is called, enumerate all S3 objects with prefix 'galleries/{gallery_id}/' and delete via s3.delete_object(). Consider rate limits for large galleries. Alternatively, move objects to 'to-delete/' folder and run separate cleanup Lambda. Ensure erasure is complete.

findings.title: Access log retention period unclear; expires_at may not be set
findings.description: The access_log table has TTL enabled with 'expires_at' attribute, but the code does not consistently set expires_at for all entries. Terraform comment says 'optional, set to 1 year' but implementation is unclear. If expires_at is not set, log entries persist indefinitely, violating GDPR Art. 5(1)(e). Users are not informed how long their email is retained.
findings.evidence: access_log_table.put_item(Item={...}) stores email and access_key but no explicit expires_at set in code. terraform/dynamodb.tf: 'TTL to auto-clean old logs (optional, set to 1 year)'
findings.fix: Ensure all access_log entries set expires_at = current_time + 365 days. Update auth_view/handler.py: expires_at = int(time.time()) + 365*86400; include in put_item(). Document 1-year retention policy in privacy policy. Consider shorter retention (90 days) if logs only needed for security audit.

findings.title: Comment data collection (email, IP, name) not disclosed to users
findings.description: When users submit comments, the app collects email, name, IP address, and comment text. However, no privacy notice or consent mechanism is shown before comment form. Users may not realize email and IP are being logged. GDPR Art. 13/14 requires transparent disclosure of what data is collected and why.
findings.evidence: submit_comment() stores email, name, ip, comment in comments_table. No privacy notice in comment form UI.
findings.fix: Add privacy notice above comment form: 'Your email and name will be visible to photographer. Your IP will be logged to prevent spam (not public). See [Privacy Policy] for details.' Consider checkbox: 'I consent to email and IP being stored as described.'

findings.title: Plain-text email addresses logged in access logs
findings.description: Access logs store plain-text email addresses. If logs are exposed (via S3 export, CloudWatch leak, or DynamoDB backup), emails are directly accessible. ISO 27001 A.8.15 recommends logs should not contain sensitive personal data. Email addresses should be anonymized or hashed.
findings.evidence: access_log_table.put_item(Item={..., 'email': email, ...}) stores plain-text email.
findings.fix: Anonymize email in logs: use SHA256(email) or one-way hash instead of plain email. Store email separately in secured table if needed for GDPR subject access requests, but don't include in routine access logs.

findings.title: Comment content insufficiently escaped; potential stored XSS
findings.description: Comments are submitted with basic control character removal but stored without HTML escaping. If a comment contains HTML/JavaScript (
findings.evidence: text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x7f]', '', ...) removes control chars but not HTML tags.
findings.fix: Implement server-side HTML escaping: use bleach (Python) to strip all HTML tags and escape entities. Alternatively, store as plain text and escape on frontend using textContent instead of innerHTML. Implement CSP with script-src 'self' to prevent inline execution.

findings.title: No comprehensive audit trail of infrastructure changes
findings.description: Terraform code is version-controlled (good), but no documented audit trail of who deployed what and when. CloudTrail may be logging AWS API calls, but it's not clear if enabled/monitored. ISO 27001 A.8.32 requires audit trails of all changes to information systems.
findings.evidence: GitHub Actions CI/CD auto-deploys on push to main, but audit trail of AWS API calls not documented.
findings.fix: Ensure CloudTrail is enabled and logging all Terraform/AWS API calls. Add monitoring/alerting for sensitive changes (IAM policy updates, S3 bucket policy changes, Lambda code updates). Document audit trail process in SECURITY.md. Consider AWS Config to track resource configuration changes.

findings.title: No published Records of Processing Activity (RoPA)
findings.description: While codebase contains data models and schemas, there is no formal Records of Processing Activity document (GDPR Art. 30) or published data inventory. Users cannot easily understand what data is collected and for how long. Art. 30 requires controllers maintain records of processing activities.
findings.evidence: No DPIA, RoPA, or data processing documentation visible in repo.
findings.fix: Create Records of Processing Activity (RoPA) or data inventory spreadsheet: (1) What data (email, name, IP, photos, comments), (2) Why (gallery access, communication, analytics), (3) How long (specify for each type), (4) Who has access (photographers, admins), (5) Where stored (DynamoDB, S3), (6) Lawful basis (contract, consent, legitimate interest). Make available to users in privacy policy or as downloadable document.

findings.title: Recommendation: Document sub-processor approvals
findings.description: Application uses third-party services: AWS (Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront), Plausible Analytics, SES. Each is a data processor. GDPR Art. 28 requires data processing agreements (DPAs). While AWS and Plausible likely have standard DPAs, there should be a documented list of processors and user notification mechanism if sub-processors change.
findings.evidence: Multiple third-party integrations but no documented sub-processor list.
findings.fix: Create 'Sub-Processors' page in privacy policy listing: AWS (data hosting, Lambda), Plausible (analytics), SES (email notifications). Include links to their privacy policies and DPAs. Provide mechanism for users to be notified if sub-processors change. This is best-practice recommendation.

findings.title: Observation: Password minimum length could be stronger
findings.description: Sub-admin password minimum length is 8 characters. While this meets basic requirements, NIST and many standards recommend minimum 12-16 characters for administrative accounts. This is a minor observation, not a violation.
findings.evidence: if len(password) < 8: return error — minimum is 8 characters.
findings.fix: Increase minimum password length for sub-admin accounts to 12 characters. For end-users (photographers/clients), 8-10 characters is acceptable. Consider adding password complexity requirements (uppercase, digit, special character) for admin accounts.

findings.title: No rate limiting on authentication or API endpoints
findings.description: There is no rate limiting on any API endpoint, including authentication routes (/api/auth/login, /api/auth/register). The API Gateway stage has no throttling configuration. This enables brute-force password attacks, credential stuffing, and API abuse.
findings.evidence: aws_apigatewayv2_stage 'default' has auto_deploy = true but no default_route_settings with throttling_burst_limit or throttling_rate_limit. The main API Lambda (lambda/api/index.mjs) has no rate limiting logic. Login failures are logged (lines 221, 230) but no counter, delay, or lockout is implemented.
findings.fix: Add throttling to the API Gateway stage: set throttling_burst_limit (e.g., 50) and throttling_rate_limit (e.g., 20/sec) in the default_route_settings. Additionally, implement application-level rate limiting for auth endpoints (e.g., 5 attempts per IP per minute) using DynamoDB-backed counters or AWS WAF rate-based rules.

findings.title: Interpret Lambda has no authentication check
findings.description: The /api/interpret endpoint (Lambda function) processes requests without any JWT token verification. While CORS restricts browser-based cross-origin requests, the API Gateway URL is directly accessible. Anyone who discovers the API Gateway endpoint can send interpretation requests, consuming Anthropic API credits and processing user-like data without authorization.
findings.evidence: The handler function (line 41) parses the event body and dispatches to interpretation handlers without any auth check. Compare to lambda/api/index.mjs line 117: 'const user = await getUser(event); if (!user) return res(401, { error: "Unauthorized" }, origin);' — the interpret Lambda has no equivalent check.
findings.fix: Add JWT verification to the interpret Lambda handler. Import the JWT verification logic from the main API Lambda (or extract to a shared module) and require a valid Bearer token before processing any interpretation request. Pass JWT_SECRET as a Lambda environment variable or use Secrets Manager.

findings.title: Consent withdrawal requires full account deletion
findings.description: The only way to withdraw consent is to delete the entire account. GDPR Art. 7(3) requires that withdrawing consent be as easy as giving it. Users cannot withdraw consent for specific processing activities (e.g., AI interpretations) while retaining their account and data.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 7: 'Withdraw consent: You may withdraw consent at any time by deleting your account.' Settings page (src/app/settings/page.tsx) only offers account deletion — no consent management options.
findings.fix: Add a consent preferences panel in settings allowing users to toggle individual processing activities (e.g., disable AI interpretations, disable analytics). Store consent preferences in the user profile and check them before processing. Update the privacy policy to reflect granular consent withdrawal options.

findings.title: Consent is bundled rather than granular
findings.description: A single checkbox on the signup form covers consent for all processing activities: account creation, data storage, AI interpretation processing, and age confirmation. GDPR requires consent to be specific to each purpose when it is the legal basis.
findings.evidence: <input type='checkbox' required ... /> 'I agree to the Privacy Policy and confirm I am at least 16 years old' — This single checkbox bundles: (1) agreement to privacy policy, (2) consent to all data processing, (3) age confirmation. GDPR Art. 7 conditions require each purpose to have separate, unbundled consent.
findings.fix: Separate the consent checkbox into distinct items: (1) 'I agree to the Privacy Policy', (2) 'I consent to AI-powered interpretations of my readings', (3) 'I confirm I am at least 16 years old'. Store each consent separately with timestamps. Make AI consent optional while the core service can function without it (just don't generate AI interpretations).

findings.title: No mechanism to restrict processing
findings.description: The privacy policy lists the right to restriction of processing (Art. 18), but no functionality exists to restrict processing without deleting the account. Users should be able to request that their data is stored but not actively processed.
findings.evidence: Settings page offers: change name, change email, change password, export data, delete account. No option to pause/restrict/freeze the account or specific processing activities. The only contact mechanism is email (privacy@thenewbeautifulme.com).
findings.fix: Add an 'Account Freeze' or 'Pause Account' feature in settings that sets a flag on the user profile. When frozen, the API should return stored data in read-only mode but not process new readings or send data to Anthropic. This satisfies Art. 18 without requiring full deletion.

findings.title: Email change without verification of new address
findings.description: Users can change their email address to any valid format without verifying ownership of the new address. This undermines data accuracy and could prevent users from exercising data rights if they enter an incorrect email.
findings.evidence: handleChangeEmail (lines 555-596): Takes new email, verifies current password, checks for duplicates, then immediately updates the email in DynamoDB and issues a new JWT. No verification email is sent to the new address. The old email is overwritten with no confirmation.
findings.fix: Implement email verification: (1) On email change request, send a verification link/code to the new email address, (2) Only update the email in DynamoDB after the user confirms via the link/code, (3) Optionally notify the old email of the pending change. This requires an email sending capability (e.g., AWS SES).

findings.title: No reference to Data Processing Agreement with Anthropic
findings.description: User questions and card selections (which may contain personal reflections) are sent to Anthropic's Claude API for processing. The privacy policy discloses Anthropic as a processor but does not reference a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) as required by GDPR Art. 28.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 4: 'Anthropic processes this data under their data processing terms.' This is vague and does not confirm a DPA is in place. GDPR Art. 28(3) requires processing to be governed by a contract/DPA that sets out the subject-matter, duration, nature, and purpose of processing.
findings.fix: Execute a Data Processing Agreement with Anthropic covering the Art. 28 requirements. Update the privacy policy to explicitly state: 'We have a Data Processing Agreement in place with Anthropic governing their processing of your data.' Anthropic offers a DPA through their enterprise/API terms — review and sign it.

findings.title: No mechanism for right to object
findings.description: The privacy policy lists the right to object to processing (Art. 21), but the only available mechanism is contacting an email address. There is no in-app functionality to object to specific processing activities, particularly AI processing of personal data.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 7: 'Object: Object to processing based on legitimate interests.' and 'To exercise these rights, contact privacy@thenewbeautifulme.com.' Settings page has no objection mechanism. While email contact is acceptable, in-app controls would strengthen compliance.
findings.fix: Add toggles in settings for: (1) 'Process my data for AI interpretations' (opt-out stops sending data to Anthropic), (2) 'Include my data in analytics/insights'. When objection is registered, immediately stop the relevant processing. This can reuse the consent preferences infrastructure from COMP-003.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline for scripts and styles
findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy includes 'unsafe-inline' for both script-src and style-src directives, weakening XSS protection. While frame-ancestors 'none' and other directives provide some mitigation, unsafe-inline allows inline scripts to execute if an XSS vector is found.
findings.evidence: content_security_policy = "...script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';..." — unsafe-inline for scripts is particularly concerning because the JWT token is stored in localStorage (src/lib/api.ts:9) and could be exfiltrated via XSS.
findings.fix: Replace 'unsafe-inline' with nonce-based or hash-based CSP for scripts. For Next.js static export, use script hashes. For styles, if Tailwind CSS requires unsafe-inline, consider using 'unsafe-hashes' with specific hashes instead. Alternatively, move to a stricter CSP using nonces (requires SSR or a CloudFront Function to inject nonces).

findings.title: JWT token stored in localStorage vulnerable to XSS
findings.description: The authentication token is stored in browser localStorage, which is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page. Combined with the CSP allowing unsafe-inline scripts, this creates a token theft risk via XSS.
findings.evidence: localStorage.getItem('tarot_token') (line 9), localStorage.setItem('tarot_token', token) (line 14). User data also stored: localStorage.setItem('tarot_user', JSON.stringify(user)) (line 35). No HttpOnly or Secure cookie attributes because cookies are not used.
findings.fix: For a static export SPA, localStorage is a common compromise. Mitigate by: (1) Tightening CSP to remove unsafe-inline (see COMP-009), (2) Implementing token rotation (short-lived access tokens with refresh mechanism), (3) Adding token binding to user-agent or IP for anomaly detection. Full mitigation requires moving to HttpOnly cookies (needs server-side rendering or a proxy).

findings.title: No account lockout after failed login attempts
findings.description: There is no mechanism to lock or throttle accounts after repeated failed login attempts. Failed logins are logged but no counter, delay, or lockout is implemented.
findings.evidence: handleLogin (lines 206-237): Failed login attempts log an event (lines 221, 230) with the reason ('unknown_email' or 'wrong_password'), but there is no counter tracking failed attempts per email or IP. No temporary lockout, CAPTCHA challenge, or exponential backoff.
findings.fix: Implement a failed login counter using DynamoDB with TTL. After 5 failed attempts within 15 minutes for the same email, temporarily lock the account for 30 minutes. Log lockout events. Optionally add CAPTCHA (e.g., hCaptcha) after 3 failed attempts.

findings.title: No Cache-Control headers on API responses containing personal data
findings.description: API responses that contain personal data (readings, journals, account info, exports) do not include Cache-Control: no-store headers. This allows browsers and intermediate proxies to cache sensitive personal data.
findings.evidence: The res() helper function (lines 37-43) only sets 'Content-Type: application/json' and CORS headers. No Cache-Control header is included. All endpoints returning personal data (GET /api/readings, /api/journals, /api/export, /api/stats) use this same response helper.
findings.fix: Add 'Cache-Control': 'no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate' to the corsHeaders function or the res() helper for all API responses. This is a one-line change in the response helper.

findings.title: Incomplete audit logging — data operations not logged
findings.description: Authentication events (register, login, password change, account deletion, data export) are logged with structured JSON, but data creation/modification/deletion operations are not logged. This creates gaps in the audit trail.
findings.evidence: Auth events logged: line 202 (register), line 235 (login success), lines 221/230 (login failure), line 551 (password change), line 674 (account delete), line 1046 (data export). NOT logged: handleSaveReading (line 290), handleDeleteReading (line 329), handleSaveJournal (line 422), handleDeleteJournal (line 451), handleShareReading (line 945).
findings.fix: Add structured logging to all data mutation handlers: console.log(JSON.stringify({ event: 'reading_save|delete', userId, readingId, ip, timestamp })). Keep log format consistent with existing auth event logging. Consider a shared logging helper function.

findings.title: No terms of service defining the contractual relationship
findings.description: The privacy policy references 'Contract' as a legal basis for processing (authentication, service provision), but no terms of service document exists to define the contractual relationship. The signup page links only to the privacy policy.
findings.evidence: Consent checkbox: 'I agree to the Privacy Policy and confirm I am at least 16 years old' — references only the privacy policy. No terms of service link. Privacy policy section 3 lists 'Contract' as legal basis for 'Authenticate your account' and 'Store your journal entries and reading history' but no contract terms are defined.
findings.fix: Create a Terms of Service page at src/app/terms/page.tsx covering: service description, entertainment-only disclaimer, user responsibilities, intellectual property, liability limitations, and governing law. Link it from the footer and the registration consent checkbox.

findings.title: Orphaned S3 images when journals expire via DynamoDB TTL
findings.description: Journal entries reference uploaded images stored in S3 via keys (e.g., '{userId}/{uuid}.jpg'). When journals are automatically deleted by DynamoDB TTL after 2 years, the referenced S3 objects remain in the bucket indefinitely. This creates orphaned personal data persisting beyond the stated retention period.
findings.evidence: Journal items store 'images: entry.images || []' (S3 keys) with 'expiresAt: Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000) + (2 * 365 * 24 * 60 * 60)' TTL. No DynamoDB Streams trigger or S3 lifecycle rule exists to clean up S3 objects when journal items expire. Account deletion (handleDeleteAccount, line 622) also does not delete S3 objects.
findings.fix: Implement one of: (1) DynamoDB Streams trigger on TTL deletes that parses image keys and calls S3 DeleteObject, (2) S3 lifecycle rule to expire all objects in the uploads bucket after 2 years, (3) Add S3 object cleanup to the account deletion handler (handleDeleteAccount).

findings.title: Age verification relies solely on self-declaration checkbox
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, CA AADC AB 2273, UK ICO Children's Code
findings.description: The minimum age requirement (16 years) is enforced only by a checkbox during signup that states 'I confirm I am at least 16 years old.' This is trivially bypassable and does not constitute robust age verification under evolving regulations.
findings.evidence: <input type='checkbox' required .../> 'I agree to the Privacy Policy and confirm I am at least 16 years old' — Client-side checkbox only. The backend (lambda/api/index.mjs line 158) checks 'if (!consent)' but this is the consent boolean, not a verified age. No date-of-birth collection, no age estimation, no third-party verification.
findings.fix: Implement a multi-step approach: (1) Replace the checkbox with a date-of-birth entry field during signup, (2) Reject users who enter a DOB indicating age <16, (3) Store DOB only for age calculation (not displayed), (4) Consider integrating an age estimation service if targeting regulated jurisdictions.

findings.title: No parental consent verification mechanism
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, UK ICO Children's Code Standard 2
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'If you are under 16, please do not create an account without parental consent,' but no mechanism exists to verify or collect parental consent. A minor could simply check the age checkbox and create an account.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 9: 'If you are under 16, please do not create an account without parental consent. If we learn that we have collected data from a child under 16 without parental consent, we will delete that data promptly.' No parental consent flow exists in the codebase.
findings.fix: If the service should remain 16+, improve age verification (see COMP-016). If serving users under 16, implement a parental consent flow: (1) Detect under-16 users via DOB, (2) Collect parent/guardian email, (3) Send verification link to parent, (4) Require parent confirmation before account activation.

findings.title: Privacy policy does not identify the lead supervisory authority
findings.description: The privacy policy mentions the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority but does not identify the lead supervisory authority or provide a link to relevant DPA resources.
findings.evidence: Section 7: 'Complaint: Lodge a complaint with your local data protection supervisory authority.' No specific DPA is named. For users unfamiliar with data protection, this is not actionable without more information.
findings.fix: Add a sentence identifying the relevant supervisory authority or providing a link to the EDPB members list for EU users. If the controller has a known establishment, name the lead DPA.

findings.title: Interpret Lambda CORS includes localhost in production
findings.description: The interpret Lambda always includes http://localhost:3000 in its CORS allowed origins list, regardless of environment. The main API Lambda conditionally includes it only in dev mode.
findings.evidence: Interpret Lambda (line 21-25): const ALLOWED_ORIGINS = ['https://thenewbeautifulme.com', 'https://www.thenewbeautifulme.com', 'http://localhost:3000']; — localhost is always included. Compare to main API Lambda (lines 17-22): uses process.env.STAGE === 'dev' conditional.
findings.fix: Apply the same environment-conditional pattern as the main API Lambda: ...(process.env.STAGE === 'dev' ? ['http://localhost:3000'] : []). Add STAGE environment variable to the interpret Lambda's Terraform configuration.

findings.title: JWT secret stored in Lambda environment variable instead of Secrets Manager
findings.description: The JWT signing secret (JWT_SECRET) is passed via a Lambda environment variable, while the Anthropic API key correctly uses AWS Secrets Manager. This inconsistency means the JWT secret is visible in the Lambda console and CloudFormation outputs.
findings.evidence: const JWT_SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET; (line 8) — Environment variable. Compare to interpret Lambda: uses aws_secretsmanager_secret for the Anthropic key (terraform/lambda.tf lines 99-111). Lambda env vars are encrypted at rest by AWS, but accessible to anyone with Lambda console access.
findings.fix: Move JWT_SECRET to AWS Secrets Manager alongside the Anthropic key. Update the Lambda to fetch it at cold start. Implement secret rotation by supporting multiple valid secrets during a rotation window.

findings.title: Privacy policy omits retention periods for some data categories
findings.description: The privacy policy lists retention for account data, readings, journals, daily card history, and AI logs. It omits retention details for: custom spreads, card notes, shared reading tokens (90 days TTL), and uploaded images.
findings.evidence: Section 6 lists retention for 4 categories. Missing: custom spreads (indefinite), card notes (indefinite), shared reading tokens (expiresAt: 90 days per lambda/api/index.mjs:976), uploaded images (may persist beyond journal deletion per COMP-015).
findings.fix: Update privacy policy Section 6 to include: 'Custom spreads and card notes: retained until you delete them or your account. Shared reading links: expire after 90 days. Uploaded journal images: retained for 2 years, consistent with journal entry retention.'

findings.title: No breached password checking
findings.description: Password validation only enforces minimum length (10 characters). There is no check against known breached password databases or common password lists.
findings.evidence: Registration (line 157): 'if (password.length < 10)' — only checks length. Password change (line 528): same minimum length check. No complexity requirements or breach database checks.
findings.fix: Add a check against the top 10,000 most common passwords (embed as a static list). Optionally integrate with the HaveIBeenPwned k-Anonymity API to check for breached passwords without exposing the password.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment documented
findings.description: The system uses AI (Anthropic Claude) to process personal questions and reflections, producing automated interpretations. This involves new technology processing personal data that could include emotional or psychological information. GDPR Art. 35 may require a DPIA.
findings.evidence: The interpret Lambda sends user questions (which may contain personal reflections, emotional concerns, relationship details) to Anthropic's Claude API. Privacy policy section 11 acknowledges this is 'automated processing that produces personalized guidance.' No DPIA documentation found.
findings.fix: Conduct a DPIA covering: (1) systematic description of AI processing, (2) assessment of necessity and proportionality, (3) assessment of risks to data subjects, (4) measures to address risks. Document as an internal file.

findings.title: No age-appropriate design assessment documented
findings.regulation: CA AADC AB 2273, UK ICO Children's Code
findings.description: No DPIA for child users or age-appropriate design assessment has been documented. While the service targets users 16+, some provisions of the California AADC and UK ICO Children's Code apply to services 'likely to be accessed by children' regardless of target audience.
findings.evidence: No DPIA documents or age-appropriate design assessments found. The service processes personal reflections and emotional data (mood tracking, journal entries) which could be especially sensitive for younger users.
findings.fix: Document a brief age-appropriate design assessment addressing: (1) whether the service is likely to be accessed by under-16s, (2) existing safeguards (age gate, privacy defaults), (3) how emotional/personal data is protected for younger users.

findings.title: No incident response or backup recovery documentation
findings.description: While the infrastructure includes backup capabilities (DynamoDB point-in-time recovery, S3 versioning) and the privacy policy mentions 72-hour breach notification, no incident response procedures, recovery runbooks, or RTO/RPO targets are documented.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 10 promises 72-hour breach notification. DynamoDB has point_in_time_recovery enabled (terraform/dynamodb.tf:38). S3 has versioning (terraform/s3.tf:12). But no incident response documentation or recovery procedures found.
findings.fix: Create an incident response document covering: (1) breach detection procedures, (2) escalation contacts, (3) 72-hour notification template for supervisory authority, (4) user notification template, (5) DynamoDB PITR recovery procedure, (6) RTO/RPO targets.
